data.title: China approves tech companies buying Nvidia H200 GPUs in some cases: report
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) shares rose nearly 2% in midday trading after The Information reported that China had approved tech companies purchasing H200 GPUs in some cases. 

The Chinese government told some local companies that H200 AI accelerators could be purchased under special circumstances, citing examples as university research or development labs, the news outlet added, citing two people with direct knowledge of the situation. The missive from Chinese government officials was also vague, as they told the companies to buy the products if it was “necessary,” but did not specify what necessary meant, the news outlet added.

Additional meetings between the Chinese government and tech companies are expected on the matter, the sources said. 

Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

China reportedly told some of its tech companies earlier this month to halt orders of Nvidia's H200 GPUs before the government made a decision on whether to allow the chips to be sold in the country.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said earlier this month week he did not believe the Chinese government will make a formal declaration that it has allowed Chinese companies to import the chipmaker's H200 GPUs. Instead, the proof would come through purchase orders.

Last year, President Donald Trump approved the sale of some advanced AI chips, including Nvidia's H200 GPUs, to China. The H200 is the predecessor to its current flagship Blackwell chips and the upcoming Vera Rubin.

Nvidia's CFO Colette Kress told analysts during a meeting at CES 2026 in Las Vegas last week that license applications for the H200 had been filed, and the government was deciding what it wants to do with them. Huang had described the demand for the chips as strong.

data.title: Morgan Stanley's PE unit takes majority stake in engineering firm Olsson
data.description:
Investment funds managed by Morgan Stanley's (MS) private equity team have made a majority investment in employee-owned engineering and design firm Olsson, Inc., according to a Tuesday statement.

Founded in 1956, Nebraska-based Olsson offers a suite of infrastructure engineering design and consulting solutions to both public and private clients across various end markets, including technology, transportation and water infrastructure, power, industrial, federal, among others.

“We are thrilled to be Olsson’s first institutional partner and believe that the Company is uniquely positioned to scale into a market leader in its targeted geographies and markets," said Eric Kanter, head of Industrial Services at Morgan Stanley Capital Partners ("MSCP"), the middle-market-focused PE unit at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.

MSCP's investment in Olsson marks its fourth investment in infrastructure services, following stakes in Resource Innovations, Apex Companies and Alliance Technical Group since 2021.

data.title: REIT stocks to watch heading into quarterly earnings season
data.description:
REITs are set to kick off the quarterly earnings season next week, with FIBRA Prologis (FBBPF) and Prologis (PLD) among the first ones to report.

The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) managed to return merely 0.15% in 2025, against the broader S&P 500 index's ~17% gain.

However, Bank of America Securities strategist Savita Subramanian recently urged investors to look beyond the broader market and focus on selective sector opportunities, particularly health care and real estate.

Bank of America’s short-term momentum and value model currently ranks real estate as the third-most attractive sector.

The strategist said the sector is not only inexpensive relative to historical market multiples, but also benefits from improving earnings revisions and a sustained period of relative outperformance, a combination that suggests genuine value rather than statistical cheapness.

"REITs delivered strong operational performance throughout 2025, weathering trade friction and continued higher interest rates, while maintaining sound fundamentals, balance sheet strength, and disciplined access to capital markets," said Nareit in its 2026 REIT Outlook, Trends, and Strategies report.

"Whether through valuation convergences, renewed transaction activity, or the expanding role of global listed real estate, 2026 has the potential to generate new opportunities for REITs and their investors," noted the report.

Below are the top five REIT stocks ranked as per Seeking Alpha's quant model, with a market cap above $2B:

  1. National Health Investors (NHI) - Strong Buy (Quant Score: 4.73/5)
  2. Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) - Strong Buy (Quant Score: 4.70/5)
  3. Agree Realty (ADC) - Strong Buy (Quant Score: 4.67/5)
  4. Kilroy Realty (KRC) - Strong Buy (Quant Score: 4.64/5)
  5. Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) - Strong Buy (Quant Score: 4.58/5)

Below are the bottom five REIT stocks ranked as per Seeking Alpha's quant model, with a market cap above $2B:

  1. Fermi (FRMI) - Strong Sell (Quant Score: 1.00/5)
  2. SBA Communications (SBAC) - Strong Sell (Quant Score: 1.22/5)
  3. SL Green Realty (SLG) - Strong Sell (Quant Score: 1.25/5)
  4. Iron Mountain (IRM) - Strong Sell (Quant Score: 1.33/5)
  5. Douglas Emmett (DEI) - Strong Sell (Quant Score: 1.36/5)

data.title: Boeing beats Airbus in jet orders for first time since 2018
data.description:
Boeing (BA) +2.8% in Tuesday's trading after reporting orders for 1,175 airplanes last year, topping the 1,000 gross sales reported by Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) previously and outselling its rival for the first time since 2018, and its net total after cancellations of 1,075 was Boeing's sixth-highest annual total and exceeded Airbus' 889 net orders.

Boeing (BA) said it handed over 63 jetliners to customers in December, the planemaker's best month since 2023 and bringing its annual delivery total to 600 aircraft, also the most in seven years, although it lagged Airbus' (EADSF) (EADSY) delivered total of 793 last year.

In 2025, Boeing (BA) delivered 440 737 MAX jets and a six-year high 88 787 Dreamliners.

Boeing's (BA) growing order book is a vote of confidence from airlines and aircraft lessors that the company's turnaround is real, Leeham aerospace analyst Scott Hamilton told Reuters.

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive emerges from difficult 2025 with a balanced risk/reward -- Wells Fargo
data.description:
After a “particularly dramatic” slowdown in fundamentals for the consumer staples sector in 2025, Colgate-Palmolive (CL) is emerging this period with a more balanced risk/reward profile. Factors which led Wells Fargo to downgrade the stock to Underweight have largely played out, leading to an upgrade to Equal Weight with a caveat that trends will continue to track below historical algorithms, precluding a more bullish upgrade.

“Ultimately, it feels too early to underwrite an emerging market bull case [for Colgate-Palmolive], with FX tailwinds in LatAm easing the need for pricing and ongoing volume struggles in Asia Pacific. Meanwhile, U.S. trends have been soft and continued below-algorithm growth seems likely to continue in FY26, as with peers,” Wells Fargo analyst Chris Carey said in a note to clients.

Valuation vs peers seems reasonable, Carey says, and the stock is the cheapest it’s been since before the pandemic, trading at a 5% discount versus the rest of the market compared to 20% to 30% historical premium.

But Carey avoids an endorsement of the stock as volume and pricing will remain pressured in FY25 – for the third time in four years – and emerging market growth, a key driver of growth for Colgate-Palmolive, will remain restrained as currency moves will create a more difficult pricing backdrop this year, Carey adds.

In tandem with an upgrade, Carey lifts his price target for Colgate-Palmolive by 12% to $86, reflecting 21.5x his CY27 EPS estimates.

Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors share a Buy rating for Colgate-Palmolive (CL), while Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating shares Carey’s neutral outlook for the stock with a Hold rating and Quant score of 2.72 out of 5.  

Colgate-Palmolive (CL) releases fourth quarter results on January 30, expected to report a profit of $0.91 per share on $5.12B in sales, unchanged and up 3.6% from a year ago, respectively.

data.title: BD invests $110M in Nebraska to boost U.S. drug delivery solutions
data.description:
• BD (BDX) on Tuesday announced a $110M investment in Columbus, Nebraska, to boost the production of its prefillable syringes designed for the administration of GLP-1 weight loss drugs and biologics.
  • Accordingly, the Franklin Lakes, New Jersey-based company, the largest medical device maker in the U.S., will allocate $100M aimed at supplying its BD Neopak Glass Prefillable Syringe from its Columbus site beginning mid-2026.
  • Another $10M will be spent on boosting cannula manufacturing capabilities at the site, BD (BDX) said, adding that both investments will create nearly 120 jobs in the region.
  • The projects are part of BD’s (BDX) $2.5B investment earmarked to expand its domestic manufacturing capacity over the next five years. They follow the company’s recently announced plans to spend $35M to increase prefilled flush syringe manufacturing at the site.

data.title: Denny's goes private as shareholders approve the buyout of 'America's Diner'
data.description:
Shareholders with Denny's (DENN) approved the restaurant company's $620M buyout by private equity investment company TriArtisan Capital Advisors (owner of TGI Fridays and P.F. Chang's), investment firm Treville Capital, and Yadav Enterprises, which is one of Denny's largest franchisees.

Looking back, Denny's (DENN) began in 1953 as a coffee shop concept that quickly shifted to a 24‑hour diner model and leaned heavily into franchising as it scaled through the 1960s and 1970s. The chain lived inside larger corporate structures and public vehicles for decades before eventually trading under its own name on the Nasdaq in 1997. Throughout the 2000s and 2010s, Denny's (DENN) management moved toward an asset‑light model, refranchising restaurants and positioning the stock as a cash‑generative, franchisor‑leaning diner brand with modest company‑owned exposure.​However, the restaurant stock lost more than 75% of its pre-pandemic high from 2019 in the challenging years that followed as the chain struggled with traffic headwinds, eventually leading the board to look at strategic alternatives.

As of June 25, 2025, the Denny's (DENN) store count stood at 1,558 restaurants, with 1,484 Denny’s‑brand locations and 74 Keke’s restaurants in the mix.

data.title: U.S. stocks slip as retail inflation comes in slightly cooler, earnings fail to lift sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Tuesday as investors assessed core CPI inflation, which came in slightly cooler than consensus, and some important earnings reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.2% in midday trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.6%.

The December Consumer Price Index came in at +0.3% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.7% YoY vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.7% prior.

Core CPI (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.6% YoY vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.

“Tuesday's CPI was in-line with expectations and that will likely keep the Federal Reserve on pause when it comes to interest rates for the foreseeable future… Recent positive employment data, elevated inflation, sticky price levels and political noise will keep the Fed at bay through at least the Spring,” said Skyler Weinand, CIO at Regan Capital.

“CPI is still under a cloud from the missing October number. Inflation is still relatively high, but trending downward. Consumers are still grappling with high overall price levels and sticker shock from the post-Covid jarring in housing, healthcare, education and everyday purchase prices,” Weinand added.

Also, new home sales slipped 0.1% MoM to 737K in October, vs. 716K expected, from 738K in the prior month, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Among companies reporting on Tuesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) -3.2% posted better-than-expected earnings, while Delta Air Lines (DAL) -2.5% had year-over-year revenue growth. 

Big companies reporting this week include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Citigroup (C).

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher to 4.19%, while the U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also unchanged at 3.54%. Investors are awaiting a 30-Year bond auction later this afternoon.

data.title: Mid to low cap industrial stocks with the strongest earnings momentum
data.description:
As the earnings season kicks off this week, we look at mid-to-low market cap industrial stocks with strong earnings momentum, which often attract investor attention as positive analyst revisions to earnings estimates can signal improving fundamentals.

The EPS Revision rating evaluates how analysts have adjusted their earnings per share estimates for a company over recent periods, with upgrades suggesting growing confidence in a company’s profitability outlook.

The following low to mid cap industrial sector stocks have earned the highest possible EPS revision rating of A+, indicating the most favorable trajectory of analyst earnings estimate changes.

data.title: Global PC shipments rose 9% in 2025; supply constraints cloud 2026 outlook
data.description:
Global shipments of desktops, notebooks and workstations rose 9.2% in 2025 to 279.5 million units, following a 10.1% increase in the fourth quarter, as per a report by Omdia.

Notebook shipments rose 8% in 2025 to 220.4 million units, while desktop shipments increased 14.4% to 59 million units. Omdia said tighter memory and storage supply emerged during the year, pushing costs higher and prompting vendors in December to signal price increases, which has already weighed on shipment expectations for 2026.

Between the first and fourth quarters of 2025, mainstream PC memory and storage costs rose 40% to 70%, Omdia said, adding that supply next year is unlikely to fully meet demand.

Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) led the market in the fourth quarter and for the full year, shipping about 71 million units in 2025, up 14.6% from the last year.

HP (NYSE:HPQ) ranked second, while Dell  (NYSE:DELL) posted its strongest quarterly performance of 2025 with a 26% annual increase in fourth-quarter shipments.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) was the fastest-growing vendor for the full year, with shipments up 16.4% to about 28 million units, while Asus (OTCPK:ASUUY) completed the top five with around 20 million units shipped.
